title: Grade 5 Activities
description: Decimals, multi-digit operations, fractions, volume, and coordinate geometry

level1.title: Place Value & Decimals
level1.goal: Understand decimal place value to thousandths, compare/round decimals, and use powers of 10.

g5-l1-big-idea.title: Big idea
g5-l1-big-idea.content: Decimals aren't "new numbers"—they're the same place-value system stretched to the right. Each step right is **÷10**, and each step left is **×10**. When kids can *see* place value, rounding and comparing become calm and logical.

g5-l1-notice.title: What to notice
g5-l1-notice.content:
* Compares by looking left to right (ones → tenths → hundredths → …).
* Can explain why 0.5 = 0.50 (same value, different places filled with zeros).
* Rounds by checking the "next digit" (and can point to it).
* Uses estimation to catch silly answers (3.9 + 4.1 is about 8).

g5-l1-home.title: Try at home
g5-l1-home.content:
* **Game A (core idea): "Place-Value Slide"** Write a number like 23.405 on paper. Say "×10" and have your child slide every digit one place left on a drawn place-value chart. Then "÷100" and slide two places right. Ask: "Which digit became the tenths digit?"
* **Game B (second representation): "Money Decimals"** Use coins or pretend money: $1.00, $0.10, $0.01. Build amounts like $2.37 and write the decimal. Ask: "How many hundredths is 37 cents?"

g5-l1-videos.title: Optional videos
g5-l1-videos.content: **Search YouTube for:** * decimal place value to thousandths grade 5 * rounding decimals number line * multiply and divide by powers of ten decimals * decimal hundredths grid model

level2.title: Multi-Digit & Decimal Operations + Expressions
level2.goal: Fluently multiply and divide multi-digit whole numbers, operate with decimals, and write/interpret expressions.

g5-l2-big-idea.title: Big idea
g5-l2-big-idea.content: Grade 5 operations aren't about "new tricks"—they're about **making place value visible**. Multiplication is area. Division is "how many groups?" Decimals behave like whole numbers **as long as you respect the places**. Expressions are just a way to *write the story* of a calculation.

g5-l2-notice.title: What to notice
g5-l2-notice.content:
* Estimates first ("24 goes into about 100 about 4 times…").
* Uses area/partial products to explain multiplication.
* For decimals, lines up decimal points and checks reasonableness.
* Reads expressions as grouped steps (parentheses = "do this together").

g5-l2-home.title: Try at home
g5-l2-home.content:
* **Game A (core idea): "Area Model on a Napkin"** Pick 2 two-digit numbers (like 34×26). Draw a rectangle, split by tens/ones, and fill partial products. Add them up.
* **Game B (division thinking): "Chunk It"** For 936 ÷ 24, ask: "What's 24×10? 24×20?" Subtract chunks until you can't subtract anymore. Count chunks = quotient.

g5-l2-videos.title: Optional videos
g5-l2-videos.content: Search for partial products area model multiplication grade 5 on YouTube. Search for partial quotients division two digit divisor on YouTube. Search for adding and subtracting decimals to hundredths on YouTube. Search for write numerical expressions with parentheses grade 5 on YouTube.

level3.title: Fractions Toolkit
level3.goal: Treat fractions as numbers on the number line and as operators. Understand fractions as division, add/subtract unlike denominators, multiply fractions, and explore division with unit fractions.

g5-l3-big-idea.title: Big idea
g5-l3-big-idea.content: Fractions are **numbers on the number line** and also **operators** ("of"). A key Grade 5 insight: **fractions ARE division** (3/4 means 3 ÷ 4). Most fraction confidence comes from making **equivalent fractions** so you can compare, add, and subtract. Multiplying fractions is easiest when you can *see the overlap* in an area model.

g5-l3-notice.title: What to notice
g5-l3-notice.content:
* Understands that 3 ÷ 4 = 3/4 ("sharing 3 among 4 gives each 3/4").
* Uses benchmark fractions (0, 1/2, 1) to estimate.
* Converts to a common denominator before adding/subtracting.
* Can explain fraction multiplication as "part of a part."
* Understands division questions as "how many groups of…?"

g5-l3-home.title: Try at home
g5-l3-home.content:
* **Game A (core idea): "Fraction Strips Race"** Draw fraction strips for halves, thirds, fourths, sixths, eighths, twelfths. Call out a fraction (like 3/4). Ask: "Show me two equivalents!" (6/8, 9/12).
* **Game B (word problems): "Tape Diagram Stories"** Write a simple story about sharing or combining fractions, then draw one bar. Label parts, then write an equation that matches the bar.

g5-l3-videos.title: Optional videos
g5-l3-videos.content: Search for fraction as division grade 5 on YouTube. Search for equivalent fractions using fraction strips on YouTube. Search for add and subtract fractions with unlike denominators grade 5 on YouTube. Search for multiply fractions area model on YouTube. Search for divide by a unit fraction visual model on YouTube.

level4.title: Measurement, Data & Volume
level4.goal: Convert units, interpret line plots with fractional measurements, and understand volume through unit cubes and formulas.

g5-l4-big-idea.title: Big idea
g5-l4-big-idea.content: Measurement is math in the real world: **units matter**. Volume is "how much space inside," and unit cubes make it visible. Line plots turn a list of messy measurements into a picture you can compute from.

g5-l4-notice.title: What to notice
g5-l4-notice.content:
* Converts units by thinking about scaling (×10/×100/×1000).
* Checks if an answer makes sense (cm vs m vs km).
* Counts volume by layers and connects to l×w×h.
* Reads line plots and can add fractional lengths without panic.

g5-l4-home.title: Try at home
g5-l4-home.content:
* **Game A (core idea): "Cube Packing"** Build a box shape with small cubes (or LEGO). Count one layer, then multiply by number of layers. Write l×w×h.
* **Game B (data): "Measure & Plot"** Measure 8–10 objects in your home to the nearest 1/2 cm (or 1/4 inch). Make a quick line plot on paper. Ask: "What's the total? What's the difference between longest and shortest?"

g5-l4-videos.title: Optional videos
g5-l4-videos.content: Search for convert measurement units grade 5 on YouTube. Search for line plot with fractions grade 5 on YouTube. Search for volume unit cubes rectangular prism grade 5 on YouTube. Search for volume formula l w h explained for kids on YouTube.

level5.title: Coordinate Plane & Geometry
level5.goal: Use coordinates to solve problems on a grid and classify 2D figures based on properties.

g5-l5-big-idea.title: Big idea
g5-l5-big-idea.content: The coordinate plane is a number line in two directions at once. Ordered pairs are "where" a point lives. **Patterns** become visible when you generate two rules and graph the pairs—you can see the relationship! Geometry is about **properties**—and properties create a "family tree" (every square is a rectangle, but not every rectangle is a square).

g5-l5-notice.title: What to notice
g5-l5-notice.content:
* Uses the "over then up" habit (x, then y).
* Checks if points line up horizontally/vertically (same y / same x).
* Sees relationships between two numerical patterns ("Rule B is always 2× Rule A").
* Names shapes by properties, not by how they're tilted.
* Can explain hierarchy statements with definitions.

g5-l5-home.title: Try at home
g5-l5-home.content:
* **Game A (core idea): "Coordinate Battleship"** Draw a 0–10 grid. Secretly pick 3 points as "ships." Take turns calling coordinates. Mark hits and misses.
* **Game B (geometry): "Property Hunt"** Find rectangles/squares in your home (books, tiles). Ask: "What makes it a rectangle? What would make it a square?"

g5-l5-videos.title: Optional videos
g5-l5-videos.content: Search for coordinate plane ordered pairs grade 5 on YouTube. Search for numerical patterns graphing ordered pairs 5th grade on YouTube. Search for distance between points same x or y grade 5 on YouTube. Search for classify quadrilaterals properties grade 5 on YouTube. Search for square rectangle parallelogram hierarchy for kids on YouTube.

fraction-scaling.title: Fraction Scaling
fraction-scaling.description: Will the product get bigger or smaller?
fraction-scaling.correctSmaller: Correct! Multiplying by a fraction less than 1 makes the product smaller.
fraction-scaling.correctSame: Correct! Multiplying by 1 keeps the product the same.
fraction-scaling.correctBigger: Correct! Multiplying by a fraction greater than 1 makes the product bigger.
fraction-scaling.wrongSmaller: A fraction less than 1 makes the product smaller.
fraction-scaling.wrongSame: A fraction equal to 1 keeps the product the same.
fraction-scaling.wrongBigger: A fraction greater than 1 makes the product bigger.

fraction-word-problems-bar-model.storyCake: A bakery had {frac1} of a cake. They sold {frac2} of the whole cake.

fraction-word-problems-bar-model.storyDistance: The trip is {frac1} of the way done. The family already drove {frac2} of the trip yesterday.
fraction-word-problems-bar-model.storyPaint: For her art project, Lily used {frac1} of a jar of blue paint and {frac2} of a jar of green paint.
fraction-word-problems-bar-model.storyBook: Sam read {frac1} of a book on Monday and {frac2} of the book on Tuesday.
fraction-word-problems-bar-model.storyWater: A water bottle was {frac1} full. Someone drank {frac2} of the bottle.
fraction-word-problems-bar-model.storyYarn: Emma had {frac1} of a ball of yarn. She used {frac2} of the ball for a scarf.
fraction-word-problems-bar-model.perfect: Perfect! You mastered fraction word problems!

measurement-reasonableness.title: Reasonableness & Units
measurement-reasonableness.description: Pick the best unit and spot impossible answers.
measurement-reasonableness.highway: A highway between two cities is about ___ long.
measurement-reasonableness.fishTank: A home fish tank holds about ___.
measurement-reasonableness.swimmingPool: An Olympic swimming pool holds about ___.
measurement-reasonableness.hintClassroom: Think: 10 cm is about as long as your hand. 10 km is very far!
measurement-reasonableness.hintPencil: Think: 18 mm is less than an inch. 18 m is taller than a house!
measurement-reasonableness.hintAnt: Think: Ants are tiny! 5 cm is about 2 inches.
measurement-reasonableness.hintHighway: Think: Cities are far apart. 50 m is shorter than a football field.
measurement-reasonableness.hintApple: Think: 200 mg is lighter than a paperclip. 200 kg is heavier than a person!
measurement-reasonableness.hintDog: Think: 15 g is about as heavy as a few coins.
measurement-reasonableness.hintPaperclip: Think: 1 kg is about a bag of flour. 1 mg is almost nothing!
measurement-reasonableness.hintCar: Think: 1500 g is only about 3 pounds. Cars are heavy!
measurement-reasonableness.hintWaterBottle: Think: 500 L could fill many bathtubs!
measurement-reasonableness.hintFishTank: Think: 40 mL is just a few spoonfuls. 40 kL could fill a pool!
measurement-reasonableness.hintTeaspoon: Think: Teaspoons are small. 5 L is about a big jug!
measurement-reasonableness.hintSwimmingPool: Think: Swimming pools are huge! 2500 L is only about 2.5 bathtubs.
measurement-reasonableness.hintBook: Think: 20 mm is less than an inch. 20 m is very long!
measurement-reasonableness.hintDoor: Think: 2 cm is about the width of your finger. 2 km is very far!
measurement-reasonableness.hintFinger: Think: 15 cm is about 6 inches - much wider than a finger!
measurement-reasonableness.hintElephant: Think: 5000 g is only about 11 pounds. Elephants are HUGE!
measurement-reasonableness.hintGrape: Think: 5 kg is about 11 pounds - way too heavy for a grape!
measurement-reasonableness.hintBathtub: Think: 300 mL is just over a cup. 300 kL could fill many pools!
measurement-reasonableness.hintEyeDropper: Think: Eye droppers are tiny. 1 L is a whole liter bottle!
measurement-reasonableness.perfect: Perfect! You have excellent measurement sense!

shape-hierarchy.instruction: Drag the shape names to build the family tree!

shape-hierarchy.perfect: Perfect! You mastered the shape family tree!
shape-hierarchy.great: Great job building the shape family tree!

always-sometimes-never.title: Always / Sometimes / Never
always-sometimes-never.description: Decide if a statement is always, sometimes, or never true.
always-sometimes-never.instruction: Is this statement always, sometimes, or never true?
always-sometimes-never.expSquareIsRectangle: Squares have 4 right angles and opposite sides equal, which makes them rectangles.
always-sometimes-never.expRectangleIsSquare: Only when all 4 sides are equal. Most rectangles have different length and width.
always-sometimes-never.expSquareIsRhombus: Squares have 4 equal sides, which makes them rhombuses.
always-sometimes-never.expRhombusIsSquare: Only when the rhombus has 4 right angles. Most rhombuses do not have right angles.
always-sometimes-never.expParallelogramHasRightAngles: Only rectangles and squares (special parallelograms) have 4 right angles.
always-sometimes-never.expRectangleIsParallelogram: Rectangles have 2 pairs of parallel sides, which makes them parallelograms.
always-sometimes-never.stmtTrapezoidHasTwoParallel: A trapezoid has 2 pairs of parallel sides.
always-sometimes-never.expTrapezoidHasTwoParallel: A trapezoid has exactly 1 pair of parallel sides, not 2.
always-sometimes-never.expQuadrilateralHas4Sides: "Quad" means 4, so all quadrilaterals have exactly 4 sides.
always-sometimes-never.expEquilateralIsIsosceles: Equilateral triangles have 3 equal sides, so they have at least 2 equal sides (isosceles).
always-sometimes-never.expIsoscelesIsEquilateral: Only when all 3 sides are equal. Most isosceles triangles have only 2 equal sides.
always-sometimes-never.stmtRightTriangleHasAcute: A right triangle has 2 acute angles.
always-sometimes-never.expRightTriangleHasAcute: A triangle has 180°. If one angle is 90°, the other two must be less than 90° (acute).
always-sometimes-never.expTriangleHas3Sides: "Tri" means 3, so all triangles have exactly 3 sides.
always-sometimes-never.stmtParallelogramHasEqualOppSides: A parallelogram has opposite sides equal.
always-sometimes-never.expParallelogramHasEqualOppSides: This is a defining property of parallelograms - opposite sides are always equal.
always-sometimes-never.expRhombusHasRightAngles: Only squares (special rhombuses) have 4 right angles. Other rhombuses do not.
always-sometimes-never.expKiteIsParallelogram: Kites have no parallel sides. Parallelograms must have 2 pairs of parallel sides.
always-sometimes-never.expSquareIsQuadrilateral: Squares have 4 sides, so they are quadrilaterals by definition.
always-sometimes-never.perfect: Perfect! You are a logical reasoning master!
